retrieved_docs.title: Bronchiectasis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Local chest examination:  Most commonly crackles and wheezes on auscultation. Crackles: 75%, usually bi-basal. Wheezing: 22% (wheezing may be due to airflow obstruction from secretions)

retrieved_docs.title: Lung Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections -- History and Physical -- Physical Examination Findings
retrieved_docs.content: The physical features on examination are often variable, not specific, and can mimic any infectious process of the lungs. Cutaneous lesions may be present in extrapulmonary disease and could represent NTM lymphadenitis. Disseminated MAC may present with hepatosplenomegaly and abdominal pain. [64] Furthermore, patients with NTM pulmonary infection may have preexisting structural lung disease, including findings of coarse inspiratory crackles on auscultation consistent with bronchiectasis or prolonged expiratory time on spirometry associated with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Bronchiectasis -- Evaluation -- Usual Pattern on CT scan of the Chest
retrieved_docs.content: Dilated bronchial arteries: These are best-demonstrated post administration of intravenous contrast. These tortuous vessels extend along the central airways toward the hila. It is these vessels that are often responsible for hemoptysis.

retrieved_docs.title: Pulmonic Regurgitation -- History and Physical -- Auscultation
retrieved_docs.content: The following may be observed on auscultation:

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Bronchitis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic wet cough for at least 4 weeks No evidence of alternative diagnoses following evaluation, including normal spirometry and chest radiography (peribronchial accentuation on chest radiography is acceptable) No signs or symptoms of alternative diagnoses Cough resolution after 2 weeks of appropriate antibiotics [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Lung Tuberculosis(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The physical exam is usually normal in mild disease or shows nonspecific lung findings such as crackles or tubular breath sounds. Absent breath sounds are noted over consolidation areas. Extrapulmonary findings include clubbing and other signs of distant organ involvement. [50]

retrieved_docs.title: Bronchiectasis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Alpha 1 antitrypsin deficiency Aspiration pneumonitis and pneumonia Asthma Bronchitis Chronic obstructive pulmonary lung disease Cystic Fibrosis Emphysema Gastroesophageal reflux disease Tuberculosis

retrieved_docs.title: Bronchoscopy -- Indications -- Diagnostic Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Hemoptysis, chronic unexplained cough, stridor, mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy, nodal staging of lung cancer, pulmonary infiltrates, pneumonia, atelectasis, suspected tracheomalacia, tracheoesophageal fistula, and post lung transplant surveillance. [3] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis Overview -- Evaluation -- Imaging
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic TB disease Cavitation Fibrosis Bronchiectasis Bronchopleural fistula uncommon

retrieved_docs.title: Hypoplastic Lung Disease -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Infectious and Postinfectious Conditions Postinfectious bronchiectasis This includes chronic airway damage following severe infections. Imaging shows dilated bronchi and scarring, unlike the rudimentary bronchial structures in hypoplasia. Tuberculosis This may cause localized lung volume loss mimicking hypoplasia.

retrieved_docs.title: Bronchoscopy -- Indications -- Diseases for Which Bronchoscopy Is Not the Optimal Diagnostic or Therapeutic Tool
retrieved_docs.content: There are diseases in which bronchoscopy is commonly done, however, the diagnostic and therapeutic yield is poor. Deshwal et al have grouped these into the following categories [7] 1. Infectious causes: Cystic fibrosis Smear negative pulmonary tuberculosis 2. Hemoptysis 3. Radiological findings Atelectasis in a patient on mechanical ventilation fibrosing mediastinitis Pleural effusion 4. Mediastinal lymphadenopathy from the following causes Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis Stage 1 Sarcoidosis Congestive lymphadenopathy Calcified lymphadenopathy Lymphoma COPD exacerbation and lymphadenopathy

retrieved_docs.title: Pulmonic Regurgitation -- History and Physical -- Auscultation
retrieved_docs.content: The first heart sound (S): This is normal.

retrieved_docs.title: Thoracic Empyema -- History and Physical -- Physical Examination
retrieved_docs.content: Auscultation : Breath sounds are diminished or absent over fluid accumulation, especially in the fibrinopurulent and organizing stages. In some cases, crackles or bronchial breath sounds may be heard at the periphery of the empyema cavity, suggesting surrounding lung consolidation. Friction rubs, though uncommon, may be audible, especially in the earlier stages.

retrieved_docs.title: Mycobacterium avium Complex -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The Mycobacterium avium complex pulmonary disease can be radiologically classified into fibro-cavitary and nodular bronchiectatic types. Case reports for atypical presentations such as pulmonary nodules, pleurisy, multiple cavitary nodules, and pleural effusion with hydropneumothorax have been published. Pleural involvement may be seen in 5% to 15% of patients. Symptoms in immunocompetent patients are nonspecific, with chronic cough as the most frequent symptom. Fever and hemoptysis are not as frequent as in tuberculosis and are uncommon in HIV patients. Fibro-cavitary disease is more common in Caucasian men, occurs in the setting of underlying structural lung disease, and presents with worsening cough, hemoptysis, and constitutional symptoms. The bronchiectasis with centrilobular nodules has a predilection for the middle lobe and the lingula. In the fibro-cavitary forms, the cavities are thin-walled with a predilection to the upper lobes. Elsewhere, there may be tree-in-bud opacities, suggesting endobronchial spread.

retrieved_docs.title: Bronchogenic Cyst -- History and Physical -- Physical Examination
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory examination: Auscultation may be normal in asymptomatic individuals. However, in symptomatic individuals, decreased breath sounds may be noted on the affected side, particularly with large cysts. Abnormal sounds like wheezing or crackles can occur if there is an associated infection or atelectasis. Percussion may reveal dullness over areas of consolidation or if a cyst is large enough to affect normal lung inflation.

retrieved_docs.title: Ciliary Dysfunction -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Some important differential diagnoses are to be considered with the pretext of primary ciliary dysfunction. [29] These include the following: Cystic fibrosis Foreign body aspiration Idiopathic interstitial pneumonia Idiopathic nasal polyposis Immunosuppression Malignancy Post-infectious bronchiectasis Severe atopy Tracheobronchomegaly

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2159
retrieved_docs.content: Computed tomography of the chest revealing a miliary pulmonary infiltrate is a radiologic finding that occurs in the lungs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 862
retrieved_docs.content: دبيلة is associated with pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse, indicating that individuals with دبيلة may develop these respiratory complications. Recognizing this relationship is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and appropriate management of patients presenting with دبيلة.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2140
retrieved_docs.content: Bronchoscopy through tracheostomy with biopsy of lung is performed with a direct procedural focus on the plíce, whereas bronchoscopy with drainage of lung abscess involves the plíce as an indirect procedural site. Additionally, a computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate identifies pathological findings situated in the plíce. Together, these interventions and diagnostic observations underscore the central role of the plíce in both therapeutic bronchoscopy techniques and radiographic assessment of diffuse pulmonary disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2659
retrieved_docs.content: Abces van long en mediastinum is identified as a finding located in the lung, indicating the presence of an abscess that may extend into the mediastinum. Bronchoscopy with drainage of lung abscess is a therapeutic procedure that, while performed via the airway, has an indirect procedural site in the lung, reflecting its role in managing pulmonary abscesses. Computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate also represents a finding in the lung, revealing a diffuse pattern of tiny nodular infiltrates that suggest widespread pulmonary involvement. Together, these entities underscore the lung as the central anatomic site for both diagnostic findings (abscess and miliary infiltrates) and interventional management (bronchoscopic drainage) of serious pulmonary infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 79
retrieved_docs.content: anatomical regions (mediastinum, pleural cavity), disease categories (other lower respiratory disease, mediastinal and pleural disorders), imaging modalities (various chest CT protocols, breast CT, pulmonary angiography), and interventional procedures (incision, thoracentesis, chest tube placement, shunt creation, thoracostomy), providing a comprehensive structure for the diagnosis, classification, and management of thoracic and lower respiratory conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2349
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on lung and bronchus are classified as a specific subset within the broader category of other diagnostic procedures on lung and bronchus, indicating that these lung‑ and bronchus‑focused diagnostic interventions fall under the larger group of related diagnostic procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 403
retrieved_docs.content: and managing diseases of the tracheobronchial tree and lung, that they are organized within a clear procedural hierarchy, and that they are directly linked to specific lung‑based malignancies and imaging findings, all of which are essential for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2425
retrieved_docs.content: Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material and Computerized axial tomography of thorax with contrast are both specific forms of a ct scan chest. The non‑contrast diagnostic CT of the thorax represents a narrower variant of the general ct scan chest procedure, while the contrast‑enhanced axial tomography is classified as a type of ct scan chest. Chest CT imaging is employed to evaluate intrathoracic pathology, and it can reveal findings such as a mass of the mid mediastinum (computed tomography of chest: mass of mid mediastinum) and a miliary pulmonary infiltrate (computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate). These relationships illustrate how different CT techniques are categorized under the broader ct scan chest modality and how they are used to detect distinct thoracic abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2132
retrieved_docs.content: The finding described as “computed tomography of chest: mediastinal effusion” is classified as an “اضطراب منصفي” (segmental disorder). This indicates that the presence of fluid accumulation in the mediastinum identified on chest CT represents a localized, segmental pathological process. Recognizing mediastinal effusion on imaging as a segmental disorder helps clinicians diagnose and manage thoracic conditions that are confined to specific anatomical regions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2184
retrieved_docs.content: The term “hrudník,” referring to the chest, denotes the anatomical location where pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse can develop. These three thoracic conditions are also linked with “دبيلة,” indicating an association between the clinical manifestations of pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse and the entity دبيلة. Understanding that the chest (hrudník) is the site of these pathologies and recognizing their connection to دبيلة is important for accurate diagnosis and effective management of patients presenting with pleural and pulmonary disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2141
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on lung and bronchus are specifically associated with the “bronchy” relationship, indicating a connection to bronchoscopy‑related techniques, and they are classified as a subcategory of the broader group termed “other diagnostic procedures on lung and bronchus.” This hierarchical arrangement clarifies that bronchoscopy‑based diagnostic methods are part of the larger set of lung and bronchus diagnostic interventions, aiding clinicians in organizing and selecting appropriate procedures for respiratory evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 1113
retrieved_docs.content: procedural instances, clarifies the anatomical focus on the tracheobronchial structure and lung, delineates methods used for tumor destruction, abscess drainage, and foreign‑body removal, and integrates the classification of bronchial‑lung malignancies, providing a comprehensive framework for bronchoscopic interventions in pulmonary care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1952
retrieved_docs.content: Acute bronchitis is classified under the broader category الْتِهابِ شعبي. Clinically, acute bronchitis is associated with الْتِهاب رِئَوِيّ. The condition الْتِهاب رِئَوِيّ includes a subcategory called التهاب رئوي وليدي. Both الْتِهاب رِئَوِيّ and the measurement حج القحف are linked to a positive فحص التوبركولين, indicating that pulmonary inflammation and head circumference may be related to tuberculin reactivity. This network of relationships highlights the hierarchical classification of respiratory inflammatory disorders and their potential association with immunologic testing results.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2419
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnostic protocol termed “Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material, followed by contrast material(s) and further sections” represents a more specific form of a chest CT scan, indicating that a non‑contrast thoracic CT is performed first and then followed by contrast‑enhanced imaging with additional sections. This refined chest CT technique is employed to evaluate thoracic pathology and serves as a method for detecting distinct radiographic findings. In particular, chest CT imaging can reveal a mass located in the mid mediastinum, as described by “computed tomography of chest: mass of mid mediastinum,” and can also identify a pattern of miliary pulmonary infiltrates, referred to as “computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate.” Recognizing these specific findings on a detailed chest CT study aids clinicians in diagnosing mediastinal lesions and diffuse pulmonary disease, thereby informing appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 108
retrieved_docs.content: bronchoscopy as the overarching procedural category aids clinicians in selecting appropriate diagnostic or therapeutic bronchoscopic approaches, aligning procedural choice with the intended clinical goal and the specific anatomic site involved.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2396
retrieved_docs.content: Bronchoscopy and biopsy of bronchus and bronchoscopy with transbronchial lung biopsy are both classified as diagnostic bronchoscopy and biopsy of bronchus procedures. These diagnostic bronchoscopy and biopsy of bronchus interventions are performed directly on the tracheobronchial structure, which serves as the procedural site.
